An intensifying area of low pressure slowed significantly as it moved into the Gulf of Maine on the 10th of February resulting in a widespread heavy snowfall across much of New Hampshire from the early morning hours of the 10th into the afternoon of the 11th. Accumulations of 2 to 33 inches were reported across the state with the highest totals occurring in central and northern portions of the state.

A major winter storm brought heavy snow, high winds, and coastal flooding to southern New England. In southwest New Hampshire, snowfall totals of 12 to 20 inches were widely observed in Cheshire and Hillsborough counties. Winds gusting as high as 45 mph created near blizzard conditions at times, making travel impossible during the height of the storm.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 21 inches in Francestown; 19 inches in Dublin, Hollis, and Nashua; 18 inches in New Ipswich and Wilton; 15 inches in Bedford and Milford; 13 inches in Alstead and South Weare; 11 inches in Hinsdale; and 9 inches in Swanzey.

A warm front associated with low pressure over Ontario crossed the region on the night of February 14th. Precipitation associated with this front changed to sleet and freezing rain across southern portions of New Hampshire, but remained in the form of snow for the entire event in north central sections of the state and ended during the morning of the 15th. Total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches.

Heavy snow fell across the higher elevations of Cheshire County, as low pressure tracked along the south coast of New England. Totals of 8 to 14 inches were common at elevations above 1000 feet, but lessened to as little as 3 to 5 inches in the valleys.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 14 inches in Marlow; 12 inches in East Alstead; 8 inches in Dublin, 5 inches in Rindge, and 4 inches in Keene and Marlboro.

Heavy snow and gusty winds affected southwest New Hampshire and all of southern New England, as low pressure reformed off the mid Atlantic coast and tracked southeast of the region. Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were widely observed.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 9 inches in Marlow, East Alstead, and Keene; 8 inches in Peterborough; 7 inches in Rindge, Hinsdale, and Greenfield; and 6 inches in Francestown, Manchester, Hudson, and Nashua.

Low pressure south of Long Island strengthened rapidly as it headed to the Canadian Maritimes, and brought heavy snow to southwest New Hampshire and much of interior southern New England. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were widely observed in Cheshire and Hillsborough Counties.State police reported numerous vehicles off roads around the region, especially on Interstate 93. A number of flights at Manchester Airport were delayed or canceled as a result of the storm.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 14 inches in Francestown; 13 inches in Alstead; 12 inches in Dublin, Marlow, Stoddard, Keene, Peterborough, and Wilton; 10 inches in Swanzey and South Weare; 8 inches in Hinsdale and Nashua; and 6 inches in Hudson and Manchester.

Low pressure strengthened rapidly off the Delaware coast and tracked southeast of New England, bringing heavy snow to southwest New Hampshire. Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were widely observed.The snow and gusty winds made travel difficult. Several roads around Manchester were closed due to icy conditions, and many spinouts were reported throughout the region. Many flights were delayed or canceled at Manchester Airport.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 9 inches in Manchester, 8 inches in Keene, 7 inches in Winchester and Hinsdale, and 6 inches in Stoddard, Bedford, Milford, and Hillsborough.

Low pressure developed in the Ohio River Valley and moved northeast through New York State.  This low spread rain into New Hampshire during the morning hours of April 23rd.  Rain was initially light but increased in intensity on April 24th as a cold front moved through. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1.5 to 2.5 inches across the area. There were several road washouts and flooded basements during this event.The Saco River at Conway rose above the flood stage of 9 feet at 1:30 pm on the 24th and remained above flood stage until 5:45 am on the 25th, reaching a crest of 12.14 feet at 9:30 pm on the 24th.The Pemigewasset River at Woodstock was above the flood stage of 9 feet from 10:30 am to 9:45 pm on the 24th, reaching a crest of 11.58 feet at 2:30 pm.  The Pemigewasset River at Plymouth rose above the flood stage of 13 feet at 3:15 pm on the 24th and remained above flood stage until 7:15 am on the 25th, reaching a crest of 15.51 feet at 11:00 pm on the 24th.

A late season storm developed off the New England coast May 25th. High astronomical tides combined with strong northeast winds and large ocean waves to produce tidal flooding and beach erosion. The tide gauge in Portland Harbor, Maine, topped out at 12.6 feet which was 0.6 feet above the flood stage of 12.0 feet.  Boulders the size of basketballs were reported to have washed up on the road in Hampton, New Hampshire where a portion of a seawall was damaged and basements were reported to be flooded.

Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and produced up to 3 inches of rain in 2 hours. This rain fell on already saturated ground causing additional road washouts and small rivers and streams to overflow.

Tremendous rainfall associated with thunderstorms moving through northern New Hampshire caused many culverts to wash out. Radar estimates of over 5 inches of rain in 3 hours in Columbia, Colebrook and Kidderville damaged many roads. Golf Links Road, and Bungy Road were all damaged. A person in Colebrook had a 5 gallon pail in his yard that was half filled in just 2 hours.

A stationary front was located through New Hampshire in an East-West orientation. Thunderstorms fired on the front during the evening hours and remained nearly stationary through Grafton county. Radar indicated 3 to 5+ inches of rain in 3 hours. The intense runoff caused much road damage in towns in southern Grafton county. One driver was injured when he drove his pickup truck through a washed out section of route 118, in Canaan. A woman was rescued by boat from her North Groton road home in Hebron when the Cockermouth River overflowed its banks. Many homes (20) were isolated until roads could be repaired due to washouts. The Groton town library flooded destroying many books, a fuel tank and new playground equipment that had just been purchased. Hanover reported many roads in the northern part of town had washed out.
Lightning produced from a thunderstorm moving through Nashua struck a middle school, causing damage to a window.  No injuries were reported.
Winds from a severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and power lines down in the towns of Warner, Hopkinton, Concord, Bow, Loudon, and Pembroke in Merrimack County.
Winds from a severe thunderstorms blew over trees in Bath and Hanover in Grafton County.

A fire ignited by a nearby lightning strike caused considerable damage to a lakeside lodge in New Hampton.  The lightning struck a nearby tree and followed the wiring of an outdoor lighting system to the lodge.

Lightning sparked a fire that destroyed a summer cottage on Lake Winnipesaukee's Badger Island.  The lightning struck a nearby pine tree and ignited a ground fire as it traveled along the ground.  The ground fire quickly spread to the cottage.

A man from Franklin was injured when lightning struck a 70-foot pine tree near where he was working in Laconia.  The man suffered burns to his leg and was transported to a nearby hospital.  The lightning ignited the tree but the heavy rains that followed the lightning strike extinguished the flames.

Lightning ignited a fire that damaged the historic Louden Town Hall which was originally built as a meetinghouse in 1779.  The second floor of the building also housed a local Baptist Church for more than 150 years.  More than 100 firefighters fought the blaze.  Lightning apparently struck the steeple and caused considerable damage to the steeple, cupola, and bell tower, with mostly water damage to the church on the second floor, and only minor damage to the first floor.

Lightning struck and ignited a fire at a home in Northfield causing extensive fire and water damage to the structure.  The lightning apparently struck the chimney, then spread to the attic of the home.  Water damage was reported throughout the structure.  Firefighters from 8 towns battled the blaze which caused an estimated $150,000 in total damage.

Lightning struck and damaged a home in Kingston.  The lightning blasted holes through the roof and outside wall of the home, and blew a 20 -foot glass window out from the interior wall and into the home.

A low pressure system moving slowly northeast from through the Mid-Atlantic States spread rain into New Hampshire during the afternoon of April 2.  Heavy rain continued through the 3rd as the storm continued to move slowly northeast.  Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches across the area with Pinkham Notch receiving 3.61 inches. The rain, in combination with snowmelt, produced numerous road washouts throughout the area and resulted in about 945 thousand dollars in damage.The Saco River at Conway rose above the 9 foot flood stage between 1:30 pm on the 3rd and 1:30 am on the 4th and reached a crest of 10.24 feet at 6:45 pm on the 3rd.The Ammonoosuc River at Bethlehem was above the flood stage of 8 feet from 12:15 pm to 2:30 pm on the 3rd and reached a crest of 8.17 feet at 12:30 pm.The Connecticut River at Dalton rose above the 17 foot flood stage between 5:45 pm and 7:00 pm on the 6th, reaching a crest of 17.14 feet at 6:00 pm. The Connecticut River at West Lebanon was above the flood stage of 18 feet from 10:30 am on the 3rd to 6:15 am on the 4th, reaching a crest of 19.34 feet at 5:00 pm on the 3rd.The Pemigewasset River at Plymouth was above the flood stage of 13 feet from 3:15 pm on the 3rd to 4:15 am on the 4th and reached a crest of 14.21 feet at 8:15 pm on the 3rd.The Smith River at Bristol rose above the flood stage of 8 feet at 9:30 am on the 3rd and remained above flood stage until 10:15 am on the 4th, reaching a crest of 8.22 feet at 5:00 am on the 4th.The Contoocook River at Henniker was above the flood stage of 12 feet from 7:00 pm on the 3rd to 4:00 pm on the 4th and reached a crest of 12.36 feet at 4:00 am on the 4th.The Warner River at Davisville rose above the 8 foot flood stage from 9:00 am on the 3rd to 9:30 am on the 5th and crested at 9.12 feet at 6:30 pm on the 3rd.

Intense runoff from thunderstorms caused road washouts and flooding of small streams. Rainfall estimates were up to 4 inches in 90 minutes. Roads affected included Twin Bridge Road, Bay Hill Road, Bean Hill Road, Rand Road, Shedd Road, Shaker Road, and Knolls Pond Road.

Lightning caused several problems in the town of Plymouth during Monday evening, June 27.  Lightning struck and ignited a fire that completely destroyed a 24- by 30-foot wooden barn in Plymouth causing an estimated $60,000 in damage.  Firefighters were able to prevent an attached home from suffering significant damage.  The Plymouth town hall was also struck and damaged.  Slate shingles and bricks from the roof and spire were blown onto the sidewalk below, and the lightning strike left a 4-foot crack in the brick structure.  A considerable amount of communications and other electronic equipment inside the building was also damaged or destroyed by the lightning strike.  Total damage to the building and electronic equipment was estimated to be about $50,000.   Also in Plymouth, a woman was struck and injured when lightning followed the well line into the basement of her home and along the concrete floor to where she was standing.  She was taken to a nearby hospital for a treatment and then released.
Lightning struck an unoccupied home that was under construction at the time and ignited a fire that caused minor damage.
Lightning struck a barn in Chichester and ignited a fire that caused minimal damage to the structure.

A 14-year-old boy was injured while playing video games on his television when lightning struck outside near the home.  The lightning apparently traveled through wires into the home.  The boy suffered burns to his hands and feet and was transported to a nearby hospital where he  spent the night as a precaution.

A trucking company employee was struck by lightning while delivering waste to a landfill.  The man was in the process of cranking a metal sky wheel to remove the nylon cover of the truck bed at the time.  The man was taken to a nearby hospital for treatment.
Lightning struck an unoccupied home in Farmington and ignited a fire that caused extensive damage to the upper story and smoke and water damage to the lower story.
Winds from a severe thunderstorm knocked over trees in several sections of Laconia.

A mother and her 2-year old son were struck and injured by a nearby lightning strike as they ran for cover from an approaching thunderstorm.  The two had been picking up toys at a Kingston Lake beach when the storm approached and were trying to reach a clubhouse when the lightning struck.  The lightning left both the mother and boy unable to move and in a semi-conscious, paralyzed state immediately after the incident, and both were taken to a nearby hospital for evaluation.

Low pressure developed in the Ohio River Valley and moved northeast through New York State.  This low spread rain into New Hampshire during the morning hours of April 23rd.  Rain was initially light but increased in intensity on April 24th as a cold front moved through. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1.5 to 2.5 inches across the area. There were several road washouts and flooded basements during this event.The Saco River at Conway rose above the flood stage of 9 feet at 1:30 pm on the 24th and remained above flood stage until 5:45 am on the 25th, reaching a crest of 12.14 feet at 9:30 pm on the 24th.The Pemigewasset River at Woodstock was above the flood stage of 9 feet from 10:30 am to 9:45 pm on the 24th, reaching a crest of 11.58 feet at 2:30 pm.  The Pemigewasset River at Plymouth rose above the flood stage of 13 feet at 3:15 pm on the 24th and remained above flood stage until 7:15 am on the 25th, reaching a crest of 15.51 feet at 11:00 pm on the 24th.

A cold front moved through New Hampshire about midday on the April 27th.  Low pressure developed on the front along the coast of Maine late in the day and produced heavy rain across the area into the morning of the 28th. Conway reported 2.08 inches of rainfall from the event.  The rainfall from this storm, in addition to rainfall from previous storms caused the Saco River at Conway to rise over its banks.  The Saco River rose above the 9 foot flood stage from 1:15 am to 10:15 am on the 28th, reaching a crest of 9.46 feet at 5:15 am.

Severe thunderstorms brought down large branches and wires in Walpole, Alstead, and West Swanzey.
A lightning strike damaged the underground electrical wires used for lighting on a ballfield in Conway.  Many of the wires had melted and about 1500 pounds of wires had to be replaced.

A hot and humid airmass combined with an approaching cold front sparked strong to severe thunderstorms across southern New Hampshire during the late afternoon and early evening hours of 27 July.  These thunderstorms produced damaging winds and dangerous lightning across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties as they moved through the region.The severe winds brought down trees and power lines across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties.  In Manchester, approximately one half dozen trees were knocked down in a cemetery from these winds.  In Mason, a 12 inch diameter tree was downed from these storms.  In addition, a lightning strike caused a barn fire in Bennington.No injuries directly resulted from these strong to severe storms.

Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southern New Hampshire, producing wind and lightning damage across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties.  In Swanzey, severe winds brought trees down along route 12, and these winds also damaged a nearby garage.  In Brookline, lightning sparked a fire at the Bingham Lumber Inc. lumber mill.  This fire caused structural and electrical damage in the building where the wood shavings are collected.No injuries directly resulted from these storms.

Lightning struck a two-story home that was under construction and ignited a fire that heavily damaged the structure.  The lightning apparently struck a nearby 70-ft tall pine tree behind the home and traveled into the building.

For the second night in a row, lightning struck a two-story home in Moultonborough.  Fire flared up about 4 hours after the initial strike and caused considerable damage to the kitchen and a new addition.
A lightning strike knocked out phone service to the town of Sanbornton's offices and caused damage to the town's computer equipment.

Lightning ignited a fire that destroyed a 200-year-old, 40 by 80 foot, 3-story barn in the town of Sandwich.  The barn had been used to store theatrical sets for a nearby theater company.  The theatrical sets, some lumber, and farm equipment were destroyed in the blaze.
Local police repoted trees down near Meredith.
A law enforcement agency reported trees down from a severe thunderstorm in Bath.
Grafton County law enforcement had numerous reports of tree limbs down on power lines in Plymouth and several surrounding towns.
A severe thunderstorm blew down numerous trees in the towns of Sandwich and Meredith.

Steel-beamed tents were tossed around and mangled when severe thunderstorm winds struck the International Speedway in the town of Loudon.
Severe thunderstorm winds reportedly downed trees in the town of Deerfield.
Thunderstorm winds reportedly blew down a large tree in the town of Epping.
In the town of Conway, lightning struck a tree and traveled underground to a nearby home.  The lightning damaged the well pump and appliances.

A hot and humid airmass combined with an approaching cold front sparked strong to severe thunderstorms across southern New Hampshire during the late afternoon and early evening hours of 27 July.  These thunderstorms produced damaging winds and dangerous lightning across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties as they moved through the region.The severe winds brought down trees and power lines across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties.  In Manchester, approximately one half dozen trees were knocked down in a cemetery from these winds.  In Mason, a 12 inch diameter tree was downed from these storms.  In addition, a lightning strike caused a barn fire in Bennington.No injuries directly resulted from these strong to severe storms.

Heavy rain fell over New Hampshire due to the rapid development of surface low pressure well to the southeast of New England. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 3 inches in southern New Hampshire up to 9.26 inches at Pinkham Notch. This resulting flooding of small rivers and streams caused additional damage to roads that had been damaged earlier in the month.

Heavy rain fell over New Hampshire due to the rapid development of surface low pressure well to the southeast of New England. Rainfall amounts across the area ranged from around 3 inches in southern New Hampshire up to 9.26 inches at Pinkham Notch. This resulted in minor flooding on the larger rivers.The Pemigewasset River crested at 9.52 ft at Woodstock (Flood Stage 9.0 ft) and at 13.73 ft at Plymouth (Flood Stage 13.0 ft). The Saco River crested at 9.35 ft at Conway (Flood Stage 9.0 ft). The Warner River crested at 9.85 ft at Davisville (Flood Stage 8.0 ft) while the Contoocook River crested at 13.75 ft at Henniker (Flood Stage 12.0 ft).  The Connecticut River crested at 13.35 ft North Stratford (Flood Stage 13.0 ft), 12.23 ft at Wells River (Flood Stage 11.0), and at 21.06 ft at Dalton (Flood Stage 17.0 ft).

The interaction between a cold frontal boundary and the remnants of Tropical Storm Tammy resulted in tremendous amount of rainfall throughout most of central and southern New Hampshire. Rainfall ranged from just under 2 inches in far northern New Hampshire to 9 inches at Gilford in Belknap County.  An unofficial report was received of 12.75 inches near Gunstock Ski area. There was a tremendous amount of damage to roads and bridges, and to the infrastructure in general due to flooding of small rivers and streams. Homes and businesses were damaged.  Tragically, two young people lost their lives in the town of Unity in Sullivan County when they attempted to drive over a bridge that had already been washed away.

A powerful cold front with a sharp temperature gradient moved across southwest New Hampshire during the late morning hours of 29 September 2005.  This cold front produced high winds that caused power outages, and knocked down trees, limbs, power poles, and wires across the region.  In particular, several trees were reported down in Keene.  In Merrimack, a tree landed on a house at 12 Garnet Lane, breaking through the roof of the house.  Several trees also came down on Wilson Hill Road, Loop Road, and Camp Sergeant Road in Merrimack.  No known injuries directly resulted from this high wind event.

Showers and thunderstorms developed across southwest New Hampshire in a hot and humid airmass ahead of a weak cold front during the late afternoon hours of 14 August 2005.  One thunderstorm became severe, producing damaging winds that knocked down numerous trees onto numerous roads in Troy.  No known injuries directly resulted from these storms.

Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southern New Hampshire, producing wind and lightning damage across Cheshire and Hillsborough counties.  In Swanzey, severe winds brought trees down along route 12, and these winds also damaged a nearby garage.  In Brookline, lightning sparked a fire at the Bingham Lumber Inc. lumber mill.  This fire caused structural and electrical damage in the building where the wood shavings are collected.No injuries directly resulted from these storms.

Lightning struck a corner of an attached garage and ignited a fire that destroyed the structure.  A motorcycle inside the garage was destroyed in the blaze.  Quick action of firefighters prevented the fire from spreading to the home, although the home did sustain smoke and water damage.

Lightning damaged equipment at Matthew Elementary School, Londonderry Middle School, and Londonderry High School. An area of low pressure developed off the mid-Atlantic coast in the early morning hours of the 9th and moved rapidly northeastward during the day.  The system brought heavy snow to portions of southern and central sections of the state.  Snowfall began in southern New Hampshire shortly before daybreak and continued into the late afternoon.  Snowfall amounts along the coast ranged from 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts (up to 15 inches) in the southeastern part of the state.  Farther north, snowfall amounts tapered off rapidly with only 3 to 5 inches across the remainder of the state.

The system brought heavy snow to portions of southern and central sections of the state.  Snowfall began in southern New Hampshire shortly before daybreak and continued into the late afternoon.  Snowfall amounts along the coast ranged from 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts (up to 15 inches) in the southeastern part of the state.  Farther north, snowfall amounts tapered off rapidly with only 3 to 5 inches across the remainder of the state.

Low pressure moving from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 25th through New England on the 26th brought a mixture of precipitation to the state.  In northern Coos County, the mixture of precipitation changed to snow with accumulations ranging from 5 to 12 inches.  Little if any snow accumulation was reported across the southern and central part of the state.

An area of low pressure that developed over the Gulf States on the morning of the 15th moved northeast to near New York City by the morning of the 16th spreading snow into the state.  As the low moved through the Gulf of Maine during the day, snow continued throughout much of the state with some sleet mixed in at times as it continued throughout much of the day.  In the southeastern part of the state, warmer air streaming northward aloft on southerly winds caused the snow to change to a mixture of rain, sleet, and freezing rain.  Snowfall accumulation ranged from 4 to 8 inches across much of the state with lesser amounts in the southeast where the snow changed to rain.

An early season storm brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and rain to the area.  Heavy snow fell in the northern mountains, especially at the higher elevations where more than a foot of snow fell.  Snow started falling shortly after noon on the 25th across northern and western sections of the state and continued through the night in the higher elevations.  Some of the higher snowfall amounts included 16 inches in Randolf, 12.5 inches at Twin Mountain, and 10 inches in Dixville.  Just rain fell across much of the central and southeastern part of the state with spotters in the higher elevations of western Merrimack and Sullivan Counties reporting 2 to 7 inches of snow.

High winds knocked down numerous trees and limbs, which, in turn, knocked down power lines throughout northern, central and southeastern New Hampshire.  About 13,000 customers lost electrical service.  Falling trees damaged vehicles and buildings, and blocked roads. Numerous trees were reported down in Berlin, Milan, and Lancaster in southern Coos County.   A tree fell on a truck in Newport while another tree fell on an SUV in Meredith.  A tree fell on a home on Route 113 in Madison.  Total property damage is estimated at about $20,000.Columbia, in Coos County, reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.  Other measured peak wind gusts included 45 mph in Meredith (Belknap County) and  46 mph in Lebanon (Grafton County).

A low pressure system interacted with a plume of tropical moisture as the low slowly moved parallel to the Long Island and south Massachusetts coasts, producing between 2.5 and 5.0 inches of rain across southwest New Hampshire.  This additional rain, combined with already swollen rivers, lead to flooding across the region.  In Milford, approximately 60 people were evacuated from low lying areas by the Souhegan River.  In Amherst, the Souhegan River overflowed its banks, flooding Starns Road, which was consequently closed.  No injuries directly resulted from this flood event.

The remnants of Hurricane Katrina caused heavy rain in northern New Hampshire. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches which caused the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock to rise above flood stage. The river crested at 10.71 feet on 09/01/05 at 1215 am.

A slow-moving cold front interacted with the remnants of Tammy, producing significant rainfall and flooding across southern New Hampshire.  Storm total rainfall ranged from between 9 and 11 inches across southwest New Hampshire; and the worst flooding occurred in the Ashuelot River basin, where the community of Alstead lies.  Approximately 1000 people were evacuated in southwest New Hampshire during these floods.The most destructive flooding occurred in Alstead, where there was loss of life, totally destroyed homes and businesses, and numerous bridge washouts.  At approximately 6 AM EST, an estimated 20 to 30 foot flood wave resulted when an embankment failed.  This flood wave traveled down Warren Brook, destroying over one dozen homes, wiping out bridges, and severely damaging many other structures.  This flash flood scoured out numerous, large sections of route 123, and downed utility poles for a five mile stretch.  Downstream, this flooding took out an old double stone-arched bridge in Walpole along the Cold River.In Hinsdale, road washouts were observed with locally severe flood damage near downtown Hinsdale.  A culvert for Kilburn Brook became blocked with tree and rock debris late Saturday night and eventually gave way to wipe out a section of Main Street (Route 119), destroying an abandoned house.Two known fatalities resulted from this significant flood event.  One Alstead man was found dead in a cornfield near the Cold River in Langdon, succumbing to the Warren River culvert breach.  He did not leave his home after being told to evacuate.  A kayaker from Antrim was missing and presumed dead; he was last seen kayaking on the North Branch River in Antrim.

Heavy rain fell over New Hampshire due to the rapid development of surface low pressure well to the southeast of New England. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 3 inches in southern New Hampshire up to 9.26 inches at Pinkham Notch. This resulting flooding of small rivers and streams caused additional damage to roads that had been damaged earlier in the month.

Low pressure moving through the Gulf of Maine began drawing colder air into a broad area of rain over New Hampshire and western Maine setting the stage for an early season snowfall over the higher terrain of northern New Hampshire. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches across southern Coos county.
A Thanksgiving Day winter storm brought heavy snow to southern Coos County.  Amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the area by the time the snow ended in the evening.
